Work only in <workspace>/code/. Run tests with: docker exec -w /app/code -e PYTHONPATH=src:. <container>_rll python -m pytest tests --tb=short -q

     )
 
 
-def rlp_encode(data: typing.Any) -> str:

     try:

+        int(raw, 16)

-    return formats.convert(rlp_data, 'prefix_hex')
 
+def _str_to_bytes(s: str, str_mode: typing.Optional[str]) -> bytes:
+    if str_mode == 'hex':
+        raw = s[2:] if s.startswith('0x') else s
+        return bytes.fromhex(raw)
+    elif str_mode == 'text':
+        return s.encode('utf-8')
+    else:
+        # str_mode is None: default to text, but auto-detect 0x-prefixed hex strings
+        if s.startswith('0x'):
+            raw = s[2:]
+            try:
+                int(raw, 16)
+                if len(raw) % 2 == 0:
+                    return bytes.fromhex(raw)
+            except ValueError:
+                pass
+        return s.encode('utf-8')
 
-def rlp_decode(data: spec.BinaryData) -> typing.Any:
-    try:
-        import rlp
-    except ImportError:
-        raise Exception(
-            'the rlp package is required for this feature, try `pip install rlp`'
-        )
 
-    binary_data = formats.convert(data, 'binary')
+def _rlp_encode_bytes(payload: bytes) -> bytes:
+    length = len(payload)
+    if length == 1 and payload[0] <= 0x7f:
+        return payload
+    elif length <= 55:
+        return bytes([0x80 + length]) + payload
+    else:
+        len_bytes = length.to_bytes((length.bit_length() + 7) // 8, 'big')
+        return bytes([0xb7 + len(len_bytes)]) + len_bytes + payload
 
-    return rlp.decode(binary_data)
+
+def _rlp_encode_raw(data: typing.Any, str_mode: typing.Optional[str]) -> bytes:
+    if isinstance(data, int):
+        if data == 0:
+            return bytes([0x80])
+        elif data <= 127:
+            int_bytes = data.to_bytes((data.bit_length() + 7) // 8, 'big')
+            return int_bytes
+        else:
+            int_bytes = data.to_bytes((data.bit_length() + 7) // 8, 'big')
+            return bytes([0x80 + len(int_bytes)]) + int_bytes
+    elif isinstance(data, bytes):
+        return _rlp_encode_bytes(data)
+    elif isinstance(data, str):
+        return _rlp_encode_bytes(_str_to_bytes(data, str_mode))
+    elif isinstance(data, list):
+        encoded_items = b''.join(_rlp_encode_raw(item, str_mode) for item in data)
+        length = len(encoded_items)
+        if length <= 55:
+            return bytes([0xc0 + length]) + encoded_items
+        else:
+            len_bytes = length.to_bytes((length.bit_length() + 7) // 8, 'big')
+            return bytes([0xf7 + len(len_bytes)]) + len_bytes + encoded_items
+    else:
+        raise Exception('unsupported type for RLP encoding: ' + str(type(data)))
+
+
+def rlp_encode(data: typing.Any, str_mode: typing.Optional[str] = None) -> str:
+    raw = _rlp_encode_raw(data, str_mode)
+    return '0x' + raw.hex()
+
+
+def _rlp_decode_raw(data: bytes, offset: int) -> typing.Tuple[typing.Any, int]:
+    if offset >= len(data):
+        raise Exception('unexpected end of RLP data')
+    prefix = data[offset]
+    if prefix <= 0x7f:
+        return (bytes([prefix]), offset + 1)
+    elif prefix <= 0xb7:
+        str_len = prefix - 0x80
+        start = offset + 1
+        return (data[start:start + str_len], start + str_len)
+    elif prefix <= 0xbf:
+        len_of_len = prefix - 0xb7
+        start = offset + 1
+        str_len = int.from_bytes(data[start:start + len_of_len], 'big')
+        payload_start = start + len_of_len
+        return (data[payload_start:payload_start + str_len], payload_start + str_len)
+    elif prefix <= 0xf7:
+        list_len = prefix - 0xc0
+        start = offset + 1
+        end = start + list_len
+        items = []
+        pos = start
+        while pos < end:
+            item, pos = _rlp_decode_raw(data, pos)
+            items.append(item)
+        return (items, end)
+    else:
+        len_of_len = prefix - 0xf7
+        start = offset + 1
+        list_len = int.from_bytes(data[start:start + len_of_len], 'big')
+        payload_start = start + len_of_len
+        end = payload_start + list_len
+        items = []
+        pos = payload_start
+        while pos < end:
+            item, pos = _rlp_decode_raw(data, pos)
+            items.append(item)
+        return (items, end)
+
+
+def _convert_decoded(item: typing.Any, types: typing.Optional[str]) -> typing.Any:
+    if isinstance(item, list):
+        return [_convert_decoded(sub, types) for sub in item]
+    if isinstance(item, bytes):
+        if types is None:
+            return item
+        elif types == 'ascii':
+            return item.decode('utf-8')
+        elif types == 'raw_hex':
+            return item.hex()
+        elif types == 'prefix_hex':
+            return '0x' + item.hex()
+        else:
+            raise Exception('unsupported types: ' + str(types))
+    return item
+
+
+def rlp_decode(data: spec.BinaryData, types: typing.Optional[str] = None) -> typing.Any:
+    if isinstance(data, str):
+        raw = data[2:] if data.startswith('0x') else data
+        binary_data = bytes.fromhex(raw)
+    elif isinstance(data, bytes):
+        binary_data = data
+    else:
+        binary_data = formats.convert(data, 'binary')
+
+    result, _ = _rlp_decode_raw(binary_data, 0)
+    return _convert_decoded(result, types)

requirements.description:
The current rlp_encode() delegates entirely to the external `rlp` PyPI package,
      which is not installed in the test environment, causing an Exception("the rlp
      package is required…") for every call. Additionally, the function signature
      does not accept a `str_mode` keyword argument, so any call such as
      rlp_encode(data, str_mode='text') or rlp_encode(address, str_mode='hex') raises
      TypeError. Similarly, rlp_decode() does not accept a `types` keyword argument,
      so rlp_decode(data, types='ascii') raises TypeError.

      These two missing capabilities affect 59 non-passing tests covering:
      - Integer encoding (test_rlp_encode_integer, 17 cases)
      - Generic encoding with str_mode (test_rlp_encoding, 7 cases)
      - Address encoding with str_mode='hex' (test_rlp_encode_address, 1 case)
      - Address+integer tuple encoding with str_mode='hex' (17 cases)
      - Round-trip decode with types='ascii' (9 cases)
      - Round-trip decode with types='raw_hex' (4 cases)
      - Round-trip decode with types='prefix_hex' (4 cases)
requirements.contract:
rlp_encode(data, str_mode=None) -> str (prefix_hex):
        Implement RLP encoding natively (no external `rlp` package dependency).
        The `data` argument may be an int, str, bytes, or a (possibly nested) list.

        str_mode controls how str values are interpreted as byte sequences:
          - str_mode='text'  : encode the str as its UTF-8/ASCII bytes.
          - str_mode='hex'   : interpret the str as hexadecimal data (supporting
                               both '0x'-prefixed and raw hex strings) and encode
                               the corresponding raw bytes.
          - str_mode=None    : default; when encoding a str without explicit
                               str_mode, treat it as text (UTF-8/ASCII bytes).
                               This default must be compatible with the existing
                               rlp_encode_address / rlp_encode_address_nonce_tuple
                               helpers which pass address strings in hex format,
                               so when the input is a recognisable Ethereum address
                               (0x-prefixed, 20-byte hex) it should be treated as
                               hex bytes even with str_mode=None.

        Integer encoding rules (per RLP spec):
          - 0             → 0x80
          - 1..127        → single byte (e.g., 15 → 0x0f, 127 → 0x7f)
          - >=128         → 0x81..0xb7 length prefix + big-endian bytes

        String/bytes encoding rules:
          - single byte in range [0x00, 0x7f] → that byte itself
          - 0..55 bytes   → (0x80 + length) prefix + payload bytes
          - >55 bytes     → (0xb7 + len_of_length) + big-endian length + payload

        List encoding rules:
          - recursively RLP-encode each element; concatenate
          - if total payload <=55 bytes: (0xc0 + total_length) + concatenated encodings
          - if total payload > 55 bytes: (0xf7 + len_of_length) + big-endian length + concatenated

        Return value: always a 0x-prefixed hex string (prefix_hex).

      rlp_decode(data, types=None) -> Any:
        Implement RLP decoding natively (no external `rlp` package dependency).
        `data` is any binary-compatible input (prefix_hex, raw_hex, or bytes).

        types controls how leaf byte-string items are converted on output:
          - types='ascii'      : decode each leaf byte string as a UTF-8/ASCII str.
          - types='raw_hex'    : convert each leaf byte string to a raw hex str
                                 (no '0x' prefix).
          - types='prefix_hex' : convert each leaf byte string to a '0x'-prefixed
                                 hex str.
          - types=None         : return leaf items as raw bytes (default).

        List structure must be preserved in the output (nested lists stay nested).
        Empty lists decode to [].
        The function must be inverse of rlp_encode for the supported type modes,
        meaning:
          rlp_decode(rlp_encode(x, str_mode='text'),  types='ascii')      == x
          rlp_decode(rlp_encode(x, str_mode='hex'),   types='raw_hex')    == x
          rlp_decode(rlp_encode(x),                   types='prefix_hex') == x
        where x is any value from the ascii_examples, raw_hex_examples, and
        prefix_hex_examples test fixtures respectively.

requirements.description:
The get_config() function catches toolconfig.ConfigDoesNotExist to handle the
      case where no config file has been created. However, the version of toolconfig
      installed in the test environment does not expose a ConfigDoesNotExist attribute,
      causing an AttributeError at import/runtime:
        "module 'toolconfig' has no attribute 'ConfigDoesNotExist'"
      This crashes every CLI command that needs to load configuration, accounting
      for 14 non-passing test_subcommand_examples tests (e.g., ctc abi, ctc aave,
      ctc ens, ctc chainlink, ctc rari, ctc llama, ctc uniswap commands).
requirements.contract:
get_config() must gracefully handle a missing or non-existent config file
      regardless of which exception toolconfig raises (or does not raise) in the
      installed version.

      Acceptable strategies (choose one that is most robust):
        a) Catch a broad exception (e.g., Exception or a known base class) when
           calling toolconfig.get_config(), and fall back to
           config_defaults.get_default_config(use_env_variables=True) on failure,
           unless the failure indicates a config parse/validation error rather than
           a missing file.
        b) Probe for toolconfig.ConfigDoesNotExist with getattr() before constructing
           the except clause, and fall back to a broad catch when the attribute is
           absent.
        c) Use a try/except around the attribute access itself so that the catch
           remains valid even if ConfigDoesNotExist is not present in toolconfig.

      Behavioral contract (unchanged from current intent):
        - If config file does not exist: print a [WARNING] to stderr and return
          the default config (get_default_config(use_env_variables=True)).
        - If config file exists but is outdated: print a [WARNING] and run
          upgrade_config().
        - Otherwise: load, validate, and return the config as before.
        - The fix must not change the return type or any other observable behavior
          of get_config().

requirements.location:
code/src/ctc/binary/ — new file signatures.py; also update
      code/src/ctc/binary/__init__.py to export the new symbols.
requirements.description:
The ctc.binary module exposes no cryptographic signature utilities.
      Six tests in tests/ctc/binary/test_signatures.py fail with AttributeError
      because the following names are absent from ctc.binary:
        - pack_vrs
        - private_key_to_public_key
        - public_key_tuple_to_hex
        - private_key_to_address
        - sign_message_hash
        - get_signer_public_key
      These functions cover ECC key derivation and ECDSA signing on the secp256k1
      curve, as used by Ethereum.
requirements.contract:
Create code/src/ctc/binary/signatures.py implementing:

      pack_vrs(v, r, s, mode='transaction') -> str:
        Concatenate a V/R/S signature tuple into a single 0x-prefixed hex string.
        v, r, s may each be provided as int or '0x'-prefixed hex strings.
        mode='transaction': the v component is placed first (v || r || s).
        The returned hex string must be 0x-prefixed.
        Example: pack_vrs('0x28', '0xda44…', '0x3b90…', mode='transaction')
                 → '0x28da44…3b90…'

      private_key_to_public_key(private_key) -> str:
        Derive the uncompressed secp256k1 public key from a private key.
        private_key may be a '0x'-prefixed hex string or a raw hex string.
        Returns the public key as a 0x-prefixed hex string encoding the 64-byte
        uncompressed public key (x and y coordinates concatenated, without the
        0x04 prefix byte).

      public_key_tuple_to_hex(public_key_tuple) -> str:
        Convert a (x, y) integer tuple representing a secp256k1 public key to
        a 0x-prefixed hex string (32-byte x || 32-byte y, each zero-padded to
        32 bytes). This is the same format returned by private_key_to_public_key.

      private_key_to_address(private_key) -> str:
        Derive the Ethereum address (checksummed or lowercase) from a private key.
        Uses keccak256 of the public key bytes to derive the 20-byte address.
        Returns a '0x'-prefixed lowercase hex address string.

      sign_message_hash(message_hash, private_key, chain_id=None) -> tuple:
        Sign a 32-byte message hash with a private key using ECDSA on secp256k1.
        message_hash and private_key may be '0x'-prefixed or raw hex strings.
        chain_id: if provided (int), apply EIP-155 replay protection when
          computing the v value: v = recovery_id + 2 * chain_id + 35.
        Returns a (v, r, s) tuple of Python integers.

      get_signer_public_key(message_hash, signature) -> str:
        Recover the signer's public key from a message hash and its (v, r, s)
        signature tuple (as returned by sign_message_hash).
        Returns the recovered public key as a 0x-prefixed hex string in the same
        format as private_key_to_public_key.

      All functions may depend on the `eth_keys` or `coincurve` or `py_ecc` packages
      if they are available in the environment. The secp256k1 library (libsecp256k1
      via coincurve, or a pure-Python fallback) should be used.

      Export all six functions from code/src/ctc/binary/__init__.py via a wildcard
      import of signatures or by explicit imports.

code/src/ctc/cli/commands/compute/rlp_encode.py (new file to create)
      and

requirements.description:
The module ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode does not exist. As a result,
      10 parametrized CLI tests (test_subcommands_have_spec, test_subcommands_have_help,
      test_subcommands_have_examples, test_subcommands_verbose_can_use_single_v,
      test_subcommand_options_use_dashes_not_underscores) for the "rlp-encode" subcommand
      are never collected and never run, because the parametrize decorator iterates over
      cli_run.command_index.values() and this entry is absent.
requirements.contract:
1. Create code/src/ctc/cli/commands/compute/rlp_encode.py with a get_command_spec()
         function that returns a toolcli.CommandSpec dict satisfying all of the following:
         - 'help': a non-None string describing the RLP encoding command
         - 'examples': a non-None value (list or dict) with at least one example invocation
         - 'args': a list of arg dicts, each containing a non-None 'help' value;
           all option names that start with '--' must use dashes (not underscores);
           the name '--verbose' must not appear alone — if verbosity is needed, use
           both '--verbose' and '-v' together as a list in the 'name' field
         - all top-level keys must be valid keys from toolcli.CommandSpec.__annotations__
         The command should expose RLP encoding for arbitrary data (strings, integers, hex
         values, or lists), leveraging the existing ctc.binary.rlp_encode() function
         (code/src/ctc/binary/rlp_coding.py:119).

      2. Register the new module in cli_run.py by adding an entry to the 'compute' section
         of command_index_by_category, mapping a tuple subcommand key (e.g. ('rlp-encode',))
         to the string 'ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode'.

requirements.contract:
1. Create code/src/ctc/cli/commands/compute/integer_command.py with a get_command_spec()
         function that returns a toolcli.CommandSpec dict satisfying all of the following:
         - 'help': a non-None string describing the integer conversion/display command
         - 'examples': a non-None value (list or dict) with at least one example invocation
         - 'args': a list of arg dicts, each containing a non-None 'help' value;
           all option names that start with '--' must use dashes (not underscores);
           the name '--verbose' must not appear alone — if verbosity is needed, use
           both '--verbose' and '-v' together as a list in the 'name' field
         - all top-level keys must be valid keys from toolcli.CommandSpec.__annotations__
         The command should provide integer conversion and display, such as converting
         integers between decimal, hex (0x-prefixed and raw), and binary representations,
         leveraging ctc.binary utilities as appropriate.

      2. Register the new module in cli_run.py by adding an entry to the 'compute' section
         of command_index_by_category, mapping a tuple subcommand key (e.g. ('integer',))
         to the string 'ctc.cli.commands.compute.integer_command'.

requirements.location:
code/src/ctc/cli/cli_run.py — run_cli() function, the except SystemExit block
      (lines 358–369)
requirements.description:
The run_cli() function wraps toolcli.run_cli() in a try/except that catches
      SystemExit and suppresses it (returns normally with exit code 0) whenever the
      CTC_CONFIG_PATH config file does not yet exist. This was intended to suppress
      errors caused by missing configuration when running data/protocol commands.
      However, it also suppresses legitimate errors that arise during the 'setup'
      command itself. Specifically, when running "ctc setup --headless --skip-db"
      without ETH_RPC_URL or --rpc-url set, async_collect_provider_metadata raises
      an Exception which toolcli converts to sys.exit(1); the except SystemExit block
      then catches this and exits with code 0. Consequently subprocess.check_output()
      sees a successful exit and test_ctc_setup__requires_rpc fails with "DID NOT RAISE".
requirements.contract:
The except SystemExit handler must NOT suppress errors that originate from the
      'setup' subcommand. When the active subcommand is 'setup' (i.e., when the user
      invoked "ctc setup ..."), a SystemExit must be re-raised (or the exception must
      be allowed to propagate as a non-zero exit code) regardless of whether the config
      file exists. For all other subcommands, the existing behavior (suppressing
      SystemExit when config file is absent) is acceptable.

      Acceptable approaches:
      - Inspect the raw_command or sys.argv to detect whether 'setup' is the subcommand,
        and re-raise SystemExit in that case.
      - Remove the except SystemExit block entirely if it is no longer needed.
      - Any equivalent mechanism that ensures: when "ctc setup" fails due to missing
        RPC configuration in headless mode, the process exits with a non-zero code so
        that subprocess.check_output() raises CalledProcessError.

      The fix must not break test_ctc_setup__env_var_sets_config_path, which runs
      "ctc setup --headless --skip-networking" and expects a successful (exit code 0)
      run even when the config file does not yet exist at the start.

requirements.description:
All five non-passed tests are parametrized over cli_run.command_index.values().
      The test IDs for ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode are "not properly
      collected or executed", which means the string
      'ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode' was absent from command_index at
      collection time (i.e. the parametrize list never produced a test case for
      this subcommand).

      Two gaps must be addressed together:

      1. The CLI subcommand module code/src/ctc/cli/commands/compute/rlp_encode.py
         must exist, be importable, and expose a get_command_spec() function that
         returns a well-formed toolcli.CommandSpec dict.

      2. The subcommand must be registered in cli_run.command_index_by_category
         under the 'compute' key, mapping the tuple ('rlp-encode',) to the module
         path string 'ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode'.  The flat
         command_index dict is derived from command_index_by_category at module
         load time, so this registration is what makes the test parametrize
         discover the subcommand.

      The binary-level function ctc.binary.rlp_encode is already present and
      tested separately (test_rlp_encoding.py passes); it does not need to be
      re-implemented.
requirements.contract:
Module: code/src/ctc/cli/commands/compute/rlp_encode.py

      • Must be importable without errors (all top-level imports must succeed).

      • Must define a function get_command_spec() -> toolcli.CommandSpec.

      • The dict returned by get_command_spec() must satisfy all of the following:

        a) Its keys must be a subset of toolcli.CommandSpec.__annotations__.keys().
           Required keys: 'f' (the handler callable), 'help', 'args', 'examples'.

        b) 'help' must be a non-None string describing the subcommand.

        c) 'examples' must be a non-None value (list of example strings or a dict
           mapping example strings to descriptions).  At minimum two examples
           should be provided so that the command is self-documenting.

        d) The 'args' list must not contain any arg whose 'name' field is the
           bare string '--verbose'.  If a verbosity flag is needed it must be
           declared with both forms, e.g. name=['--verbose', '-v'].

        e) Every arg whose name (or each name in a name list) starts with '--'
           must use hyphens, not underscores, in the option name
           (e.g. '--str-mode' is valid; '--str_mode' is not).

      • The module must also define the command handler function referenced by
        'f'.  It accepts the positional argument 'data' (str) and an optional
        keyword argument for the string mode flag.  It must call
        ctc.binary.rlp_encode and print the result.

      Registration: code/src/ctc/cli/cli_run.py

      • command_index_by_category['compute'] must contain the entry:
            ('rlp-encode',): 'ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode'
        This causes command_index (built at module load time) to include the
        mapping, which in turn makes the parametrized tests discover and run the
        five rlp_encode test cases.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
All five of the following test cases must pass:

      tests/ctc/cli/test_cli_subcommands.py::test_subcommands_have_spec[ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode]
      tests/ctc/cli/test_cli_subcommands.py::test_subcommands_have_help[ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode]
      tests/ctc/cli/test_cli_subcommands.py::test_subcommands_have_examples[ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode]
      tests/ctc/cli/test_cli_args.py::test_subcommands_verbose_can_use_single_v[ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode]
      tests/ctc/cli/test_cli_args.py::test_subcommand_options_use_dashes_not_underscores[ctc.cli.commands.compute.rlp_encode]

      Verification steps:
      1. The test node IDs above must appear in the collected test suite (i.e.
         pytest --collect-only must list them), confirming the registration is
         present.
      2. Each test must execute without error or assertion failure.
      3. No previously passing tests in test_cli_subcommands.py or
         test_cli_args.py may regress.
